header.title: Maternity leave — entitlement, pay, risk assessment, return to work

body.summary: Maternity rights are more extensive than most people realise — knowing them early means you can plan properly and protect yourself.
body.text:
TOPIC: Maternity leave
DURATION TARGET: 4–5 minutes
ELEVENLABS VOICE SUGGESTION: Speaker A — "Aria" (warm, slightly nervous female), Speaker B — "Sienna" (calm, reassuring senior female)

[SPEAKER A]: Can I run something past you? I'm about ten weeks pregnant, and I haven't told anyone at work yet. I keep worrying that the minute I say something, they'll think I'm less reliable, or start planning to replace me. Part of me wants to just keep my head down for as long as I can.

[SPEAKER B]: First of all, congratulations. And I'm really glad you came to me. Let me say the important bit straight away: it is against the law for us to treat you worse because you're pregnant or because you're going to take maternity leave. You're protected from the day you tell us. So please don't carry that worry on your own.

[SPEAKER A]: That's good to hear. But honestly, what does "protected" actually mean day to day?

[SPEAKER B]: It means we can't push you out, sideline you, or pick on you because of the pregnancy. If your job were ever at risk through something like redundancy while you're pregnant or on leave, you'd get extra protection on top of everyone else. If anything ever felt unfair, you tell me or HR, and we deal with it. You should never feel you have to hide this to stay safe.

[SPEAKER A]: Okay. So when do I actually have to tell you officially?

[SPEAKER B]: You can tell me whenever you feel ready — earlier is genuinely better for you, and I'll explain why in a second. The formal deadline is by about fifteen weeks before your due date. Once you tell me, two good things kick in. First, I have to do a risk assessment for you. Second, we get your leave and pay sorted properly.

[SPEAKER A]: A risk assessment? I'm a care worker, not on a building site.

[SPEAKER B]: I know, but think about the moving and handling, long shifts on your feet, infection risks. Your manager sits down with you, looks at your actual duties, and we adjust anything that could be a risk — maybe lighter tasks, or sharing the heavier lifting with the team. And we review it as the pregnancy goes on, because things change trimester to trimester. If you have any advice from your midwife or GP, bring it and we'll work it in.

[SPEAKER A]: That's reassuring. What about leave and money? I haven't been here that long and I'm scared I won't qualify.

[SPEAKER B]: Two separate things, and this trips a lot of people up. The leave itself — up to a year off — you get that regardless of how long you've worked here. No qualifying period. The pay is different. Statutory maternity pay has eligibility rules around how long you've worked and your earnings. It runs for a set number of weeks, paid at a higher rate at the start and then a flat statutory rate after that.

[SPEAKER A]: And if I don't qualify for the pay?

[SPEAKER B]: Then you may be able to claim maternity allowance through the Jobcentre instead, so you're not left with nothing. I'd genuinely steer you to check the current figures with HR, because those amounts are set nationally and they can change each year. I don't want to quote you a number that's out of date.

[SPEAKER A]: Makes sense. One last thing — once I'm off, that's it, I'm cut off?

[SPEAKER B]: Not at all, unless you want to be. There are keeping-in-touch days — a small number of optional days where you can come in for training or a catch-up without ending your leave or losing pay. Plenty of people like staying in the loop. And when you come back, you've got the right to return to your job. If you wanted different hours afterwards, you can request flexible working — just put it in early, ideally about eight weeks before you're due back.

[SPEAKER A]: That's such a weight off. I think I was bracing for a fight.

[SPEAKER B]: No fight. This is normal, it's planned for, and we want you to come back. Take it one step at a time, and lean on me and HR whenever you need to.

---
KEY TAKEAWAYS (for show notes / text card pairing):
1. You're entitled to up to a year of maternity leave no matter how long you've worked here, and you're protected from unfair treatment from the moment you tell your employer.
2. Notify your manager by around the fifteenth week before your due date — this triggers a pregnancy risk assessment and gets your leave and pay set up.
3. Pay has eligibility rules and a flat statutory rate after the early weeks — check the current figures with HR — and ask about keeping-in-touch days and your right to return.
